title: Colour at the Edge — Goethe and the Threshold Where Seeing Becomes Active
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe

body.text: Goethe'scolour theory begins with areversal. Colour is not aproperty sitting inside objects. It appears at the boundary — between light and darkness, eye and world, transparency and opacity. It is aphenomenon of encounter, athreshold where the world and the organ meet.
Light and darkness, brightness and obscurity, or if amore general expression is preferred, light and its absence, are necessary to the production of colour. Colour itself is adegree of darkness.

Adegree of darkness. The phrase is ametaphysical instruction. Colour is born from polarity — it is neither pure light nor pure darkness, but the threshold where one becomes perceptible through the other. The rendered world is not made of isolated objects. It is made of relations. Colour teaches the eye to notice the in-between.

body.caption: Colour as threshold — the interval where light and darkness meet.

body.text: If the eye sees acolour, it is immediately stimulated to produce the opposite colour.

The eye answers the world. Acolour calls forth its opposite. The organ participates in the completion of the phenomenon. The afterimage shows that the perceiving body has its own lawful activity. This is not fantasy — it is exact observation of experience.

body.caption: The afterimage — the eye completing the world through its own law.

body.text: The eye may be said to be indebted to light for its existence. Out of indifferent animal organs, light calls forth an organ that shall be its like.

The eye is formed by light and for light. The organ and the world secretly belong to one another. This is the deepest implication of Goethean science: the knower and the known are not strangers. The organ has been shaped by what it meets. Perception is areunion.

Colour is the first proof that seeing is active. Look at acoloured surface. Look away. Notice the answering colour arising in the eye. Return to the world and observe how perception has already become participatory.
